description: The smooth transitions of power that NYC has enjoyed ensure that adaptation projects and relevant agencies are not overly disrupted.
The government of NYC includes a number of city-level agencies focused on adaptation initiatives.

As one of the largest cities in the United States, NYC requires an appropriately large budget. The city appropriates large amounts for capital projects aimed at resilience and adaptation. These projects also receive funding from Federal grants.

The healthy economy of NYC allows adaptation measures to be implemented without exerting an undue burden on residents and businesses, while providing the City with the revenue necessary to implement such projects.

The majority of buildings in NYC were built pre-1961, making them generally less resilient, and, in some cases, more difficult to retrofit. Many utilities are located at or below ground level, putting them at risk during flood events and heat waves. In addition, much of the City’s transportation infrastructure, including its subway system, was not built with resilience in mind, and are in need of high levels of maintenance and repairs.
The underlying factors are cost (total costs and who pays) and siting challenges due to the scale and density of the infrastructure that exists in NYC.

The high cost of living in NYC has pushed many low-income residents to neighborhoods in the floodplain, which have historically been considered less desirable due to the presence of industry and distance from the city center. This trend has put low-income communities at greater risk of storm surges and inundation. The high cost of living also prohibits many residents from purchasing retrofits and flood insurance.
Mayor is a champion for climate action and adaptation which helps drive policies and action implementation through the city.
Enhances as this is the vehicle for which we can implement actions (data collection will be implemented through land use strategies)

Inequality in socioeconomic status, quality of housing, and exposure to climate risks (i.e. high heat) increases individuals' inability to adapt. To address this, the L.A. prioritizes adaptation projects in the most vulnerable communities.
Limited housing and poor housing infrastructure presents a challenge to the city, particularly as it relates to preparing for natural disasters such as earthquakes.
Challenges our ability to adapt as poor infrastructure acts as a vulnerability, particularly in the case of natural disasters (i.e. earthquakes)
Hinders adaptive capability of populations made vulnerable by a lack of quality housing or housing at all

Inequality in socioeconomic status, quality of housing, and exposure to climate risks (i.e. high heat) increases individuals' inability to adapt. To address this, the L.A. prioritizes adaptation projects in the most vulnerable communities.
Water constraints present a challenge when adapting to high heat (and fires as a result of heat and drought)

Chicago has access to a clean, large source of freshwater. Thus, Chicago is relatively protected from droughts and other extreme issues related to water scarcity. Further, Chicago, and Illinois generally, is an energy rich region, which adds to stability and security in the face of a changing climate. Nuclear power and renewable energy combined comprise close to 60% of net energy generation. Chicago is generally a food secure City, however, equitable food access continues to be a challenge. According to Feeding America’s Map the Meal Gap Study, one in seven people in Cook County will experience food insecurity this year.Sources:- https://www.eia.gov/state/print.php?sid=IL- https://www.chicagosfoodbank.org/hunger-in-our-community/- https://www.chicago.gov/content/dam/city/sites/food-equity/pdfs/City_Food_Equity_Agenda.pdf

Inadequate housing stock for BIPOC and low-income communities as a result of historic red-lining challenges the City's ability to adapt to climate impacts like heat, flooding, and major storms. The Chicago Departments of Planning and Development, Housing, and the Mayor's Office are currently in the process of assessing affordable housing climate resiliency standards nationwide in an effort to deliver policy recommendations to boost housing climate resiliency.

Chicago is home to world class academic institutions who provide regular and substantive support to the City's various departments. Many of our adaptation studies and existing conditions analyses rely on expertise from supporting research organizations like the University of Illinois at Chicago, Northwestern University, the University of Chicago, and other institutions.

Chicago's Department of Public Health has done an excellent job of public engagement with COVID-19 data. Further, their recent work addressing racial inequities and the impact on Black Chicagoans has paved the way for even more progress on communicating public health data to the public in digestible formats. There is reason to believe that these projects can and will be translated to our climate action work, especially related to resilience.Chicago's Office of Emergency Management and Communications (OEMC) also partners with various City departments to enhance climate resiliency. Specifically, OEMC has a running list of cooling and heating centers as well as an outreach strategy around major weather events that threaten heath and well-being. Sources:- https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/oem/supp_info/alertrespond/extremetemps.html

The Chicago Department of the Environment was dissolved under the previous administration. The staff have since been reallocated to different agencies, but this separation has created barriers to more effective climate mitigation and resiliency planning.The Chief Sustainability Officer (housed in the Mayor's Office) is in the process of rebuilding a core, coordinating, team for environmental and climate work.

There is limited government capacity to fund projects,  materials, and emergency personnel. City government budget cuts have led to many vacant positions being eliminated and forced some departments to continue to operate short-handed. Houston lacks regulatory authority to make changes in electricity market, emissions control, transportation system, and other sectors.

Political engagement/ transparency becomes another conflict when the issue itself becomes politically polarized by party politics. Political involvement withdraws when monetary donations to politicians and government agencies are at risk. Political involvement withdraws often to promote business and political motives nullifying intervention or regulatory changes that can help the cities to mitigate causes. Transparency slows the city’s ability to adapt when FEMA or other government agencies remove informative text and material from their websites that hinder education to citizens.

Budgetary capacity becomes problematic when the importance of adapting the city to climate change is not prioritized.. For three consecutive years the City of Houston has faced budget cuts, approximately $80 million each year, due to the revenue cap, hurricane recovery spending, and COVID-19-related impacts. In 2021, the budget gap reached approximately $200 million, but was filled with federal recovery funds that came just in time. The budget shortfalls that the city has faced has made it difficult to undertake projects requiring capital, even if these projects would improve efficiency and save the city money in the long run. In some cases, lack of budgetary capacity directly prevents the city and region from undertaking necessary adaptation measures. Refineries around the Houston Ship Channel and Galveston Bay produce 40% of the nation's petroleum and half of the country's jet fuel. Storm surges and flooding in this region pose two grave risks: 1) Serious environmental contamination, and 2) huge shortages of petroleum products.  As a result of Hurricane Ike in 2008, 500,000 gallons of crude oil were spilled causing $29 billion in damages. A group of universities and partners have led the planning for the "Ike Dike," a defensive barrier designed to protect this key area. The project would extend an existing sea wall, construct a 17 ft revetment near the beach and add flood gates. Gates and barriers would likely be modelled after those used in the Netherlands' Delta Works project. The project is estimated to cost $15 billion and has been stalled due to lack of funding. This project is necessary to protect public health and national security as a hurricane wiping out oil production ability in the region could cause fuel shortages and greatly impair transportation in the United States.

Rapid urbanization over the years, with the population doubling in the last 50 years along with unsatisfactory infrastructure capacity, building code regulations, and inadequate land use planning has caused streets, channels, river, dikes, drainage ditches, levees, dams and reservoirs to flood or overflow and cause extreme flooding throughout the Houston area. Additionally, rapid development and urbanization has exacerbated the extreme heat and heat wave impacts

Houston's topography and soil type contribute to it being a flood prone region. The area is low lying and has clay-based soil with low permeability. The area has historically relied on natural wetlands and bayous for drainage. However, when severe storms occur these natural protections are unable to handle the inundation of water.

Storm water infrastructure in Houston poses major difficulties. As Houston is located in a low-lying region with a clay-like soil that naturally has low-permeability, the region's bayous have functioned as the main drainage system, but many neighborhoods must have engineered storm water management systems in place. Aging infrastructure can limit the effectiveness of storm water drains. Additionally, as the frequency and intensity of storms increase, it becomes clear that the engineered capacity of such systems may not have been high enough to face the increased intensity of storms. Limited storm water capacity can lead to flooded streets impairing residents' ability to evacuate in emergency situations. The American Society of Civil Engineers 2017 Report Card gave the State of Texas a C- for infrastructure noting that the state earned a D for flood control and roadways.

The City of Houston is the largest city in the United States to not have zoning laws in an effort to encourage development. Largely unobstructed either by rules or by natural features such as mountains, the Houston area sprawled. Nearly 25,000 acres of natural wetlands were developed between 1992 and 2010, mostly in Harris County. Between 1992 and 2010 alone nearly 30% of wetlands in Harris County were destroyed due to development. The region is low lying and with soil that is mostly clay-based, causing the region to be naturally prone to flooding and drainage problems. The area's bayous have largely functioned as  storm water management and drainage systems. However, development in the area and over wetlands threaten this natural drainage mechanism. As Houston is rapidly expanding-- the population in Houston and the surrounding cities is expected to grow by 66.7% by 2040-- land use planning poses a huge challenge to manage the cities development and drainage needs, and to adapt to changing flood plains.
Continued economic growth has allowed the community to invest in green technologies and promote more sustainable business models.
As the City of Phoenix increases in population, urban sprawl has increased, increasing demand for resources such as water, energy and transportation.
Phoenix continues to actively manage its water resources to ensure a one-hundred year water supply.
Challenges due to extreme weather, like extreme heat events, are met by partnerships with local NGO and other local, state and federal agencies to assist impacted populations.
Philadelphia has the highest poverty rate of any large city in the United States, limiting the adaptive capacity of many of our residents.

Many of the neighborhoods most impacted by climate stressors like extreme heat and sea level rise are communities of color, which face disproprtionately high rates of unemployment, sub-standard housing, and other environmental justice issues stemming from historic  and existing inequities in our city.
Philadelphia residents broadly (more than 80%) support climate action, and there is political consensus  toward fighting the impacts of climate change locally.
Adaptation efforts will require individual and community action, which the Office of Sustainability and community partners have been piloting through our Beat the Heat pilot.

The combination of flash flooding and extreme heat will rapidly degrade the City's infrastructure and historical assets. Without improvements to the city's infrastructure it will be costly to replace in the upcoming decades.
The increasing level of sprawl will exacerbate the transportation issues and make it more difficult to transition to alternative modes of transportation.
The level of poverty in San Antonio impacts a family's ability to adapt to extreme heat (e.g. AC costs) and its ability to access city resources.
With the combination of a growing population and extreme heat, the lack of water and its increased value will have an impact on San Antonio.

San Antonio is projected to have 24 additional days of 100 degree weather by 2040. The combination of a growing population and disappearance of green spaces, extreme heat, and potential lack of water, exacerbates the effects of urban heat islands and public health issues.
Consequences of climate change disproportionately affect vulnerable populations. Equitable solutions and strategies to coping with climate change are being developed.

All facets of state and local government are committed to address climate change and plan for mitigation and resiliency. Support and direct access to resources from the Federal level have recently increased.
N/A
Low-income communities of color with limited access to healthcare may face adaptation challenges related to air quality, extreme heat, flooding, and other climate-related public health hazards.
Individuals living below the poverty level have limited access to adaptation resources.

Older stormwater infrastructure has limited capacity to mitigate the effects of severe flooding events.  Electricity and water outages during extreme weather events increase negative impacts during those episodes.
The City's drinking water comes from one source (Highland Lakes - surface water), which makes the City extremely vulnerable to prolonged drought events.

Rapid development, especially in the City periphery, places a greater number of residents at risk of flooding and wildfire hazards. Development in the Wildland-Urban Interface increases the risk of wildfire impacts.

The cost of housing in Austin is currently at an historic high. This will affect the City's efforts to place a growing homeless population into long-term affordable housing and prevent the exodus of Austin's low-income and communities of color.

Inequality (Environmental Equity) limits the ability of the economically challenged to adapt to additional extreme heating days, flooding, with limited access to energy conservation and food supply. The impacts of a changing climate will be felt the most by vulnerable communities, such as the elderly, communities of color, the very young, and the disadvantaged.
Environmental factors will lead to increased stress on existing infrastructure and increase the costs of maintenance and new infrastructure

As the climate changes, the availability of resources such as water /food will be increasingly stressed.  We have found that the Dallas’ food shed exceeds 2,500 miles – impacts to the transportation infrastructure, as well as agriculture itself, can impact availability and costs for food.

As the population of Dallas continues to grow, new demands will be placed on infrastructure, such as roads, grid capacity, natural gas supply and others.  Maintenance and expansion of the infrastructure must be included in climate and sustainability planning.

Infrastructure for the delivery of health services and for general economic well-being, including health care facilities, water treatment plants, and roads for emergency responders and transportation for health care personnel, can be damaged in climate-related extreme weather events.
Increased burden of disease and injury could test the surge capacity of health care facilities.

Economic disruption from climate-related hazards (such as the current COVID-19 pandemic) can lead to income loss, income insecurity, food insecurity, housing insecurity, and mental health problems, which in turn may increase substance abuse, suicide and other health problems. All these challenges require City attention and resources, limiting the resources available for climate adaptation.

Continued affordable housing shortages in San Jose and California result in longer commutes for lower- and middle-income working families and increased transportation emissions. Housing shortages can also increase vulnerable populations, such as people experiencing homelessness. Both residents and the City invest substantial resources in housing, reducing the resources available for other needs, such as climate adaptation.

The City’s governance structure is stable. The current Mayor and City Council are strong supporters of Climate Smart San Jose. The City partners with other local governments and regional and state agencies to share resources and knowledge .

As the Capital of Silicon Valley, the City benefits from the tech sector’s continued growth. This results in a culture of innovation, and increased human capital and strategic partnerships to address climate challenges. For example, Google has received Council approval to develop Downtown West, a transit-oriented community of office buildings, homes, restaurants, shops, public space, and parks on about 80 acres in downtown San Jose.

The success of the City's climate mitigation and adaptation plans depends on actions by residents as well as by businesses and the City itself. However, many residents are struggling financially and cannot afford to take adaptation actions such as installing battery storage, air conditioning or planting trees.

Community-based organizations in our city are increasingly showing interest and concern about climate change mitigation and adaptation. Their engagement with the City on climate change helps build the political will for the City to take action.

The City budget now includes two permanent positions and three term-limited positions to support the Climate Smart San Jose program and coordinate climate change actions and efforts across the City organization.This work is also supported by climate change research and policy work by regional and state government agencies.

Infrastructure throughout Indianapolis has been underfunded and the conditions pose a challenge for adapting to climate change. The overall operation and maintenance of levees are considered minimally acceptable to acceptable,  many are not maintained with best practices, some may be obsolete, and only 23% are accredited by FEMA.  As for streets, officials estimate it will cost "$732 million for city streets to be upgraded to fair condition from the current rating of poor. To keep the streets in fair condition — a 4 on a 10-point scale — another $178 million a year would be needed for upkeep, more than double the current annual funding that Indianapolis has available for all roads, bridge and sidewalk projects" (Indy Star, 2/15/18). The City's storm water management infrastructure also needs significant funding and maintenance.

With 20% of our population living in poverty, the number of people facing this challenge is much higher than the national average of 13%. Additionally, approximately 10% of these persons are experiencing extreme poverty, so community resilience is limited.

Indianapolis is well-known for its health institutions including Riley Children’s Hospital, IU Health, Sidney and Lois Eskenazi Hospital, among others. Many specialized options exist for those seeking treatment for a particular ailment, thus positioning Indianapolis well to handle a potential increase in insect transmitted disease and heat-related disease brought on by increasing average temperatures. This potential is also on the minds of the Marion County Health Department.
This cost of living in Indianapolis is relatively low compared to urban areas in surrounding states.
The City of Indianapolis Office of Sustainability has tabled at  many events and completed extensive community outreach related to Thrive Indianapolis to help the city adapt to climate change.

City residents, businesses and visitors are fortunate that critical city organizations, community institutions generally have been very proactive in addressing climate change issues.For example, American Electric Power Ohio and Columbia Gas of Ohio have taken steps to prepare for climate change by taking measures to implement grid resilience (electric) and repair old infrastructure (gas lines). The U.S Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) recognized AEP Ohio and Columbia Gas of Ohio with a 2016 ENERGY STAR Partner of the Year—Sustained Excellence Award and a Market Leader Award for continued leadership in protecting the environment through superior energy efficiency. The utilities collaborate to encourage energy efficiency within the home, including building more than 10,100 EfficiencyCrafted homes that have reduced carbon emissions equal to that sequestered by more than 625,500 acres of trees for one year according to the EPA. https://www.aepohio.com/info/news/viewRelease.aspx?releaseID=1970The City of Columbus Department of Public Utilities (DPU) realizes that the effects of climate change will likely lead to an increase in the demand for basic services. To support the city’s adaptation efforts, Columbus DPU is taking steps to repair and replace old power, water, and sewerage and drainage infrastructure, as well as installing new infrastructure.In the past two years (2019-2020, the Division of Power, which is a unit of DPU has repaired more than 229,000 feet of wire and cable and installed/removed a total of 16,351 transformer KVA. Additionally, at least 4,350 luminaries and nearly 19,345 lamps have been repaired and more than 1,200 total wooden and standard poles have been replaced since 2018, according to the 2020 Columbus Department of Public Utilities annual report.In the past three years, the Division of Water, also a part of DPU, has made 6,045 repairs of water infrastructure, according to the department’s annual report. Division of Water is currently updating its Master Plan which will include information on the timeline of the development of the 4th water plant for the city, recognizing that planning for additional water supply is critical as the impacts of climate change increase.

The Columbus Health Literacy Committee was assembled by the Columbus Public Health Department to help Columbus residents gain easy access to healthcare and is composed of various healthcare and health insurance organizations from around Central Ohio. The group meets once a month to work towards its’ mission to “raise awareness about the impacts of health literacy on residents, providers, and organizations, and create easy-to-understand information to help individuals navigate their health care options.”  In addition, the City of Columbus has started the Celebrate One initiative to put in place recommendations from the Greater Columbus Infant Mortality Task Force to reduce the community’s alarming infant mortality rate by 40 %  and cut the racial health disparity gap in half by 2020.

Columbus has the 3rd lowest dropout rate for high school attendance, is in the middle tier of metros with a population with either a bachelor’s or graduate degree, and ranks 4th in school lunch assistance
The overall cost of living in the Columbus Region is on average 10% more affordable than the national average and lower than the State of Ohio despite rapid growth.

The Affordable Housing Alliance of Central Ohio Report lists access to affordable housing as a challenge of the central Ohio Region.   To address this challenge, MORPC, the City of Columbus, and other regional partners are coordinating a housing strategy for Central Ohio.  https://www.morpc.org/program-service/regional-housing-strategy/The Columbus Metropolitan Housing Authority also helps people access affordable housing by working with partners to develop, renovate, and maintain housing, promote neighborhood revitalization, and assist residents in accessing needed social services. CMHA Goals for FY 2016-2021: (1) Continue to increase the availability of decent, safe, and affordable housing; (2) Promote self-sufficiency and asset development of families and individuals; (3) Improve community quality of life and economic vitality; and (4) Ensure Equal Opportunity and Affirmatively further Fair Housing. CMHA will endeavor to realize the goals of the 2016 5-Year Plan through the execution of the Choice Neighborhood Implementation Grant which focuses on the revitalization of the Near East Side of Columbus, converting Public Housing through the Rental Assistance Demonstration Program, utilizing the PBV allocation to support development and the continuation of the provision of high quality program administration. In January 2016, CMHA opened Poindexter Place - a104 unit elderly development with 34 Public Housing and 70 Project Based Voucher units. Construction has begun on Poindexter Phase II – 87 affordable units with 35 PBV, 21 LIHTC and 31 market rate units. CMHA was recently awarded tax credit funding for Poindexter Phases III and IV.

According to 2019 statistics from the U.S. Census Bureau, the poverty rate in Columbus, Ohio is 20.4 percent.The City has identified several low-income neighborhoods where energy poverty is a significant issue i.e. residents in these neighborhoods pay an unaffordable amount of their income (6-11%) on utility bills. In city partnership with utility companies AEP Ohio and Columbia Gas, the Community Energy Savers (CES) program was created to assist those opportunity neighborhoods in realizing the cost-saving benefits of energy efficiency in their homes. Through this program, home energy audits and weatherization upgrades are offered for free or reduced cost to residents. If each of the opportunity neighborhoods hits their goal AEP Ohio and Columbia Gas will grant them money to upgrade a community asset; ex. Water heaters in schools. Reducing the amount of income spent on utility bills and wasted energy helps to alleviate poverty in Columbus.  http://communityenergysavers.com/

In 2015, Columbus had an income gap ratio of 18.3, with the top 1% of earners making an average of $989,323 and the bottom 99% of earners making an average of $54,097. This places Columbus at 255 of 916 for cities with the greatest income disparity. Economic inequality between city neighborhoods is a significant challenge in Columbus, and one often intersects with race. In our poorest neighborhoods crime is concentrated and access to jobs and transportation is lacking. Areas like Linden, Hilltop, and the South Side also suffer from high infant mortality rates. Additionally, African-American residents in these neighborhoods are far less likely to own cars and spend a large amount of valuable time commuting to work.Areas like Linden, Hilltop, and the South Side also suffer from high infant mortality rates. Additionally, African-American residents in these neighborhoods are far less likely to own cars and spend a large amount of valuable time commuting to work. The Smart Columbus initiative aims to address this inequality and improve access to opportunity by bringing innovative transportation solutions to the neighborhoods that need it most. The initiative, supported by a $50 million grant from the U.S. Department of Transportation, will enhance human services with projects like: Prenatal Trip Assistance, Mobility Assistance for people with Cognitive Disabilities, and a Multimodal Trip Planning App with a Common Payment System. The Community Energy Savers program is also operating within these neighborhoods to reduce the burden that energy costs pose to low-income households. Efforts to reduce the racial and economic disparities between city neighborhoods with help us to overcome this challenge and better adapt to a changing climate. http://communityenergysavers.com/https://www.smartcolumbusos.com/about/about-smart-columbus

Like many areas, the unemployment rate increased in 2020 due to covid. Columbus averaged 7% unemployment rate in 2020. However the last three months of 2020 saw the unemployment rate hover around 4%.  https://data.bls.gov/pdq/SurveyOutputServlet

Columbus has high infant mortality rates, high levels of adult obesity (30.6% of population haves a BMI of 30 or greater), but ranks 9th in access to local foods, and is in the middle tier for percentage of adults diagnosed with asthma.The CelebrateOne program monitors data on Franklin County birth rates, infant mortality, and other key indicators to track progress towards ensuring that all babies—regardless of race, zip code or family income—celebrate their first birthdays.The CelebrateOne “Social Determinates of Health Data Brief” identifies vulnerable population and priority areas within Columbus to improve overall health. Priority areas include: Linden (43211), Northeast (43219), Northland (43224, 43229), Hilltop (43204), Franklinton (43222, 43223), Near East (43203, 43205), South Side (43206, 43207), and Southeast (43227, 43232). Identifying areas of improvement and working towards progress in these neighborhoods will help the entire community adapt to the changing climate.https://www.columbus.gov/Celebrate-One/Infant-Mortality-Data/.

Columbus has a stable political environment.   The state and national political environment, however, does create challenges.  At the state level, the passage of HB 6 in 2019 which eliminated IOU energy efficiency program funding and rollbacked the state's renewable energy portfolio standards has caused significant instability in these markets and threatens Columbus' ability to meet its long term climate goals.  At the federal level, as of May 20, 2020, there are at least 100 environmental rules and regulations that are being rolled back under the Trump administration, according to a story in the New York Times.  It has been suggested that if all the rollbacks go into effect, greenhouse gas emissions could increase significantly resulting in poorer air quality and thousands of increased deaths. SInce President Biden was elected the environmental roll backs have stopped at the federal level.

Mayor Ginther and the Columbus City Council are committed to taking action to adapt to the evitable impacts of climate change and mitigation green house gas emissions.  In 2019, under Mayor Ginther's leadership the City has legislated for a Renewable Energy Owner's Representative to explore renewable procurement options to serve the City's electricity load and legislated to update the City's Climate Action to include GHG reduction goals beyond December 2020. Columbus' Sustainable Columbus 2030 plan has a vision of 100% renewable energy provided by community aggreagation by 2022, carbon neutral by 2050, and prosperous neighborhoods with green space, clean air, and safe drinking water.  The State of Ohio has robust public records laws that require city governments to operate in a transparent manner.  The City of Columbus goes above and beyond these laws by actively engaging stakeholders in City government processes.  Columbus is nationally known for its stakeholder engagement.  We believe in shared leadership, which means that we not only seek input from a broad range of stakeholders when we make decisions, but we also involve them in the very earliest stages of planning and have them work with us to make programs successful.  We have two very active sustainability advisory boards, the Sustainable Columbus Advisory Board and GreenSpot Advisory Board.  When we developed our Green Community Plan, stakeholders form 34 different organizations contributed.  We continued this tradition when we partnered with The Ohio State University to develop a Climate Adaptation Plan and engaged over 89 stakeholders from a variety of organizations.  To develop the City’s next Climate Action Plan, the city has engaged with stakeholders from throughout the community as well as the public in a virtual setting even as engagement was complicated by the impact of the COVID-19 worldwide pandemic.

City of Columbus provides critical services to its residents.   In 2020, it treated 70 billion gallons of waste water, provided 35,000 immunizations and collected trash for 340,000 households, and owned 402 parks for its residents to enjoy.

The City of Columbus has a well-rounded operating fund budget.  In 2020 the total operating budget was $1.96 billion (all funds), with $965 million in the general fund, according to city budget documents.  The City's reliance on the income tax as a source of operating budget does result in vulnerabilities in the event of economic downturns, however,  Columbus has a rainy day fund that with a goal level of 75M, which has been achieved.

The Mid-Ohio Regional Planning Commission (MORPC) predicts that 1 million additional people will live in the Central Ohio/Columbus region by 2050. Population growth exposes more people to the risks involved with climate change. This will make climate adaptation more difficult, as additional resources will need to be allocated to provide aid during extreme weather events.  Increases in temperature and changing precipitation patterns could negatively affect agricultural production and increase the prices of food in the US. As the population increases, a greater number of Columbus residents will be at risk of hunger and place strain on the adaptive capacity of the city to assist those in need.

Columbus Community Safety Advisory Commission was created in November 2017 by Mayor Andrew J. Ginther to address safety concerns involving police misconduct in Columbus’s low-income neighborhoods. This Commission is focused on areas such as de-escalation, crisis intervention, and implicit bias training; use of force policies; diversity recruitment and retention; and early intervention and officer wellness programs.https://www.columbus.gov/safetycommission/.  City-wide safety is addressed in both state and city emergency management plans. Under the Extreme Heat Plan, Franklin County Emergency Management and Homeland Security (FCEM & HS) will coordinate information flow between various agencies, community partners, and the general public during an extreme heat emergency. The same agency has developed Emergency Support Function 1 (ESF1), which is responsible for management of transportation systems and management of transportation infrastructure during threats or in response to flooding.At the state level, the Ohio Emergency Management Agency operates to ensure safety during an emergency event. One example of their work is the Drought Assessment Committee and Impact Task Force which appropriately matches state response to the severity of a drought. Under this plan “local jurisdictions may enact ordinances to assure equitable water distribution and may establish local drought emergency public information and education programs.” Emergency management activities during a regional drought emergency would be coordinated between Franklin County Emergency Management and Homeland Security and leadership of the City of Columbus, including the Department of Public Utilities, Division of Water.

Columbus ranks No. 1 for its affordability and access to job opportunities. The Columbus economy is balanced with a combination of education, technology, government, research, insurance and health care entities as major employers within the City. Columbus is gaining nationwide recognition for its booming downtown, historic neighborhoods, arts and sporting districts, open attitude and a noticeably affordable quality of life.Major employers include: The Ohio State University, Limited Brands, Inc., and Nationwide Insurance.The Smart City Challenge $50 million grant to explore, demonstrate, and test technologies and infrastructure of the future was grown to $500 million with additional public and private funding. These partnerships will be the key to future economic development in the city. The innovative nature of our programs makes Columbus, America’s Opportunity City. The Smart City initiative proves that cities don’t have to choose between economic growth and combatting climate change. Investing in these technologies now will help Columbus’s economy grow and improve our adaptive capacity.

Columbus has a well-rounded employment industry. The largest employment sectors are Professional & Business Services, Government, Education & Health, and Retail Trade. Each industry will be affected by climate change in a different way. A diverse economy means that climate risk will be spread out and the city as a whole will be less vulnerable. https://www.columbus.gov/development/economic-development/Employment-By-Sector/

Columbus is experiencing rapid growth. https://www.census.gov/newsroom/press-releases/2019/subcounty-population-estimates.htmlThe Mid-Ohio Regional Planning Commission (MORPC) predicts that 1 million additional people will live in the Columbus region by 2050. Population growth exposes more people to the risks involved with climate change. This will make climate adaptation more difficult, as additional resources will need to be allocated to provide aid during extreme weather events.  Increases in temperature and changing precipitation patterns could negatively affect agricultural production and increase the prices of food in the US. As the population increases, a greater number of Columbus residents will be at risk of hunger and place strain on the adaptive capacity of the city to assist those in need.

Columbus has a plentiful water supply source flowing from several major bodies of water around the city, including: the Olentangy River, the Scioto River, Alum Creek, and the Hoover Dam and Hoover Reservoir. The historical water inventory indicates that there is currently a surplus of approximately 493,000 million gallons of water within the Upper Scioto River watershed. As part of the Sustaining Scioto Adaptation Plan, a future water inventory for the Upper Scioto River Watershed, under climate change conditions, was determined for the years 2035 and 2075. Five out of eight models predicted reduced water availability in both 2035 and 2075, whereas three models indicate an increased water availability for the region. This balance is significantly impacted by the projected increased water demand and water discharges from the water and wastewater treatment plants. It is important to note that this inventory calculation was developed to provide only an overview of potential comprehensive impacts to water availability within the watershed.  As data sets change and calculations are revised, continuing to run these models will help Columbus to expect and plan for future variations in precipitation and water availability. Planning a framework of development using water inventory and population growth models will support the city’s adaptive capacity in the future. http://www.morpc.org/wp-content/uploads/2017/12/2014WaterInventory.pdf

Columbus has high quality water resources and is home to one of the most modern water quality research laboratories in the water supply industry, the Water Quality Assurance Laboratory (WQAL). At WQAL certified personnel perform thousands of tests each year and provide data regarding water quality, treatment, and microbiological testing. This ongoing testing and research assures that Columbus drinking water will be of the highest quality, currently meeting all Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) standards.The city’s air quality has steadily improved since 2012.  In 2019, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) declared that Columbus, OH was the first city to move out of non-compliance with federal air quality standards for ground-level ozone, after three years of monitoring data showed the air has finally reached safe levels.

Columbus has a comprehensive plan that established policies for Columbus’ growth, development, and redevelopment to 2010 and has specific land use plans for neighborhoods). https://www.columbus.gov/planning/ColumbusLandUseRecommendations/.  The Mid-Ohio Regional Planning Commission published an Insight2050 Corridor Concepts report in April 2019 focusing on land use planning strategies that could be used to facilitate focused growth along 5 corridors in Central Ohio resulting in GHG reductions, transit benefits, and other community assets.  With the support of Columbus City Council, leadership teams within the City Administration are now evaluating this land use plan and translating this plan into implementable action plans.

The Columbus GreenSpot program engages the community by inspiring, educating, and recognizing households, businesses, and community groups that adopt green practices. Environmental behavior change is a necessary step towards developing support for action against climate change and supporting the city’s adaptation efforts.  In 2020, enrollment in the program topped 20,000 members since the program’s start in 2008. These households have registered for GreenSpot membership and agreed to green their everyday lives through a number of commitments. Collectively the whole membership saved more than $13 million, reduced CO2 emissions by 41 million pounds, reduced water consumption by 145.8+ million gallons, and recycled 32.4 million pounds.  Households receive a bi-monthly newsletter, containing green tips, events, invitation to sustainability-related outings, etc. in 2019.  The GreenSpot Community Backyards program provided 596 $50 rebates in 2019 on rain barrels, and native plants and native trees. Participants first must learn about the impacts of stormwater and ways to prevent polluted runoff.One hundred forty three community groups have committed to engaging sustainability. These groups include community gardens, area commissions, classrooms, churches, and conservation groups.One thousand three hundred and twenty businesses have taken the pledge which requires progress towards commitments to be environmentally friendly in five categories: Inform and Engage, Reduce/ Reuse/ Recycle, Conserve Water, Conserve Energy, and Green Transportation.Some businesses are invited to participate in the GreenSpot Sustainability Initiative class. They attend 6 monthly classes where they receive hands-on, professional support and recommendations on ways to make their organization more sustainable. The class offers the businesses a chance to network and share ideas amongst themselves.All members enjoy a GreenSpot window decal. High achievers, as well as the graduating Sustainability Initiative Class are recognized at the annual GreenSpotLight awards where they received special recognition from Mayor Ginther and city council. The collective climate awareness of GreenSpot individuals, businesses, and groups will help to support the cities adaptation efforts.  https://www.columbus.gov/greenspot/

In 2016, Columbus won a $40 million Smart City Challenge grant from the US Department of Transportation (USDOT). Another $10 million was granted by Vulcan Inc. to create smart energy solutions for the city. As defined by the International Data Corporation (IDC), smart city development is “the use of smart initiatives combined to leverage technology investments across an entire city, with common platforms increasing efficiency, data being shared across systems, and IT investments tied to smart missions.” While this program is very new, the established Smart Columbus Operating System (SCOS) is at its core. SCOS is an online, open-source data platform that contains various city data, including traffic conditions, electrical grid status, and transit user ship. The data collected and stored here will soon manage and connect the Smart applications, vehicles, and other infrastructure outlined in the Smart Columbus Final Program Management Plan. The City of Columbus’s expectations for this data and the program as a whole are to: Improve Safety, Enhance Mobility, Enhance access to Opportunities & Services, and Reduce Environmental Impact. The ability to collect city data and analyze the changing needs of residents and businesses in real-time, will support Columbus’s climate change resilience and adaptive capacity. https://www.smartcolumbusos.com/https://smart.columbus.gov/uploadedFiles/SCC-A-PMP-Final-Program%20Management%20Plan_1-30-18.pdf

The City of Columbus Division of Water maintains 3,539 miles of water line, 37 water tanks, 25 booster stations, 3 in-stream reservoirs, 1 up-ground reservoir, and 26,000 fire hydrants. In 2017, the Division of Water responded to 3,752 work orders regarding water lines, mains, valves, and hydrants. The Division did $24 million of existing distribution infrastructure improvements through its Replacement and Rehabilitation (R&R) Program. Major R&R Projects replaced 74.000 feet of water mains. The Division of Sewerage and Drainage maintains 4501 miles of sewers. 2017 also saw late stage construction of $265 million dollars of capacity increase and treatment upgrade projects across multiple waste water treatment plants. Com-Til, the city’s composting program which converts solid waste from water treatment plants to agricultural fertilizer, began plans for infrastructure maintenance and update planning to continue this program for at least the next 30 years.

In 2019, The City of Columbus provided drinking water to nearly 1.2 million consumers in the city and contracting suburban communities, and has a capacity of 255 million gallons a day sanitary sewer system. In 2019, the Division of Water legislated over $25 million in existing distribution infrastructure improvements through its Replacement and Rehabilitation Program. This program annually prioritizes replacement of water mains that require repeated maintenance due to breakage and the need to improve flow to service areas. Major replacement and rehabilitation projects in 2019 included: Dewberry Drive Area Water Line Improvements, Dresden Street Area Water Line Improvements, Union Avenue Area Water Line Improvements. The Division of Water also legislated over $5 million to replace transmission mains. These larger diameter water mains are critical in providing water to every customer and could cause wide spread water disturbances if damaged. Replacing and adding transmission mains ensures the infrastructure is reliable and adds redundancy. Major transmission main projects include: Olentangy River Road 24” Water Main Project; Mound District Booster Station 20” Discharge Line Project; Henderson Road Booster Station; Westgate East Tank Replacement.

In 2020, the city passed the first building energy benchmarking ordinance which requires energy consumption reporting for large buildings. It requires requirements covered, city buildings and non-city properties greater than 100,000 square feet to report by June 1, 2021. Other covered properties greater than 50,000 square feet are due to report by the following June. Public data disclosures for each group will begin in 2022 and 2023. Anticipated reduction in their carbon emissions is between 3-4% just from the reporting-many business will realize that they may not be as efficient as they thought and will take actions to reduce their climate footpring even further.

There is a clear income disparity within Charlotte, this is evident in the nature and type of housing stock alongside a variety of economic statistics. There is a great need for education on how to become more resilient as individuals and as a community. This requires careful thought on engagement processes to build trust across our community notably those in lower income groups that are likely to be disproportionately affected by events that may be attributed to climate change.

Charlotte is experiencing rapid growth with measurements indicating that up to 60 people are moving to the city every day. This has resulted in rapid development. This is causing changes to land-use which has the potential to impact both emissions of greenhouse gases as well as our capacity to adapt. The city is in the process of producing a Unified Development Ordinance within which sustainability and resilience will strongly feature. The city is investing in people in this regard and has advertised for a Resilience Fellow who will operate out of the City Manager’s office.

The city is increasingly working with it stakeholders and has seen the benefits of doing so. This engagement has been a key feature in the production of our Strategic Energy Action Plan. It is our contention that with appropriate engagement at the right time in the right places, we have the necessary channels to engage across our community and can see the areas where we need to strengthen this.The Charlotte Future 2040 Comprehensive Plan is our shared, comprehensive vision to guide the Queen City’s growth over the next 20 years. Recently adopted, the plan is the foundation for strategic policy, equitable investment in infrastructure, and new regulatory tools such as the Unified Development Ordinance. The Unified Development Ordinance (UDO) is a regulatory tool meant to guide future development so that it results in the types of community and places defined by the Charlotte Future 2040 Comprehensive Plan policies. Both of these plans incorporate energy and sustainably aspects and completed numerous phases of community engagement, specifically in marginalized communities. Comprehensive Plan Engagement: https://cltfuture2040.com/past-engagement/ Comprehensive Plan Equity Atlas: https://cltfuture2040.com/equity-atlas/
Covid 19 has affected tax income.
Covid 19 has caused greater unemployment in Charlotte and beyond.

Our governance structure is stable and results in good working relationship between the national and city governments in relation to climate change in both resourcing and knowledge sharing. City Council greatly supports sustainability work.
Lack sufficient housing supply. Challenges with affordable housing and ensuring new affordable units are low carbon.
Some vulnerable communities are experiencing higher levels of unemployment which limit a communities ability to be resilient in the event of disasters.

There are inequities in all aspects of the community and these inequities mean that some groups are more resilient and can adapt to a changing climate, whereas other communities may not have the capacity (e.g. high income household that can pay for air conditioning versus a homeless person living on the street during high heat events).

Portland already has a maintenance backlog for infrastructure. Resiliency planning has already identified infrastructure weaknesses and issues with access to critical infrastructure in the event of disasters - like landslides or flooding which could cut off bridges and other transportation routes to move emergency equipment.
We have allowed for development in flood plains and on steep slopes.

San Francisco is in a housing affordability crisis with housing costs that have increased far faster than inflation since the late 1990s and become acutely expensive during the economic boom after 2011. Due in part to lack of affordable housing coupled with insufficient overall housing production and an influx of higher income households, the city has seen an increase in cost burdens and a drop in low- and moderate-income households, certain racial/ethnic groups, and household types. Simultaneously, San Francisco has a long-standing commitment to invest in housing affordable at low and moderate incomes and to protect tenants with local ordinances on rent control and just cause eviction. Despite these efforts, the city has struggled to substantially improve housing affordability for low and moderate-income households. For households earning 30% or less of Area Median Income, more than 80% of households are rent burdened (spending more than 30% of income on housing costs) and more than 60% experience severe rent burden (spending more than 50% of income on housing costs). Between 1990 and 2015, the total number of severely rent burdened households in San Francisco increased from roughly 38,000 to 49,000. In 2019, the people counted who are experiencing homelessness is a similar number to 10% of the total population in San Francisco. On January 24, 2019, there were 8,011 people experiencing homelessness in San Francisco, a 17% increase over the 2017 Point-in-Time Count. A six-year trend of comparable Point-in-Time Count data identified a 14% increase in the number of persons experiencing homelessness in San Francisco between 2013 and 2019. http://hsh.sfgov.org/wp-content/uploads/FINAL-PIT-Report-2019-San-Francisco.pdf

The 2019 Community Health Needs Assessment identified racial and ethnic inequality as one of two foundational issues. The median income in San Francisco varies greatly by race/ethnicity. Typically, Whites earn 4x more than Black/African Americans in San Francisco. 35% of the homeless persons are Black/African American and 22% are Latinx compared to 5% and 15%, respectively, of the city overall. Black/African American Students: 13% are proficient or above in mathematics, 19% in English language arts; Latinx students: 22% of are proficient in math, 28% in English language arts; Pacifica Islander Students: 19% of are proficient in math, 25% in English language arts; White Students: 70% are proficient in Mathematics, 77% in English language arts. https://www.sfdph.org/dph/hc/HCAgen/2019/May%207/CHNA_2019_Report_041819_Stage%204.pdf

The 2019 Community Health Needs Assessment identified poverty as one of two foundational issues. Enough income generally confers access to resources that promote health — like good schools, health care, healthy food, safe neighborhoods, and time for self-care — and the ability to avoid health hazards — like air pollution and poor quality housing conditions. An estimated 10 percent of San Francisco residents were in poverty in 2017. Older residents (those 65 and over) are more likely to be in poverty than other age groups. Poverty rates also vary by race and ethnicity; most notably, Black and African American residents experience poverty at nearly three times the average rate. Women still experience poverty at a higher rate than men. https://sfgov.org/scorecards/safety-net/poverty-san-francisco

San Francisco’s General Obligation (GO) bond rating acts as the City’s credit rating and is a measure of the overall financial stability of the City. In order to fund large capital projects, the City issues bonds, or debt, and the purchase of those bonds provides the financing for these capital projects. The city’s high credit ratings allow the local government to issue debt at lower borrowing costs, while presenting minimal credit risk. The city’s operating revenue growth, and the long-term strengthening in the city’s economy will allow it to plan funding for climate adaptation projects. More information available on the finance scorecard: https://sfgov.org/scorecards/finance

While an abundant natural resource base, environmental amenities, a seaport location, a highly educated workforce, and a critical mass of globally recognized, export-oriented companies have enabled the Seattle region’s economy to flourish over the last half-century, the benefits of this significant growth have not been realized by everyone. Average household income is the fifth highest of the top 30 largest cities in America, but approximately 20% of Seattle’s people of color live in poverty, compared to approximately 9% of Seattle’s white residents. In terms of income inequality, Seattle ranks 18 out of the 50 largest U.S. cities.7This income inequality is exacerbated by a persistent gap in access to opportunity for Seattle residents. Seattle is the epicenter for much of the global innovation that is happening today: from Amazon, Boeing, Starbucks, Microsoft, Costco, Expedia, Alaska Airlines and more. The area has seen a 21% increase in jobs since 2011, but we know that many Seattle residents do not have access to these high-paying jobs that require a college degree. Only 26% of black/African American, native Hawaiian/other Pacific Islander, Latino, and American Indian/Alaska native students in Washington State earn a college degree compared to 46% of their white counterparts.

Seattle’s location and rapid population boom have resulted in unique risks and vulnerabilities that impact our ability to maintain and deepen our city’s resilience. Over the last decade, Seattle has been the fastest-growing big city in America, growing from 608,660 people in 2010 to 730,400 in 2018.2 This growth has brought with it an amazing amount of diversity, adding to our city’s vibrant and inclusive communities. More than 108 languages are spoken in our schools.The Seattle housing market, however, has struggled to keep pace with this significant growth. Our beautiful natural terrain—with Puget Sound bordering Seattle on the west and mountains, rivers, and lakes providing natural borders on all the other sides—presents one of our city’s greatest challenges. Building new housing for our growing population and keeping housing affordable are difficult without the space to expand. Housing construction only grew by 13% from 2010 to 2018 , causing home prices to jump nearly 93% over the roughly decade-long period between the bottom of the housing crisis and January 2018.As a result, one in six Seattle households is severely burdened by the cost of housing, spending more than half its income on housing, and we have seen growing rates of homelessness over the last decade. A recent report from the University of Washington disclosed that the amount a family of four needs to “scrape by” in Seattle has risen 86% from 2001 to 2017

Denver is currently experiencing a housing affordability crisis, with tremendous economic growth, low unemployment, and widespread involuntary displacement due to gentrification pressures. Housing and transportation costs combined average 42% of take-home pay. Renter households are cost burdened at much higher rates than homeowners. The impacts of climate change in Denver, including more extreme heat days, drought, and hail, will potentially widen the gap between those who have more access to resilience resources and those who don't.

The impacts on human health from climate change include those related to poor air quality, such as respiratory illnesses like asthma, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes. These will result in increased health provider visits and emergency room visits, costing patients money that would otherwise be spent on housing, utilities, transportation and food.  Also, those who lack reliable transportation will face greater logistical and financial barriers to more frequent visits to doctors, hospitals, and pharmacies.

Poverty is a significant determinant of one's ability to respond to extreme climate impacts such as heat, flooding, hail, and drought. Those with low incomes have less access to air conditioning, personal vehicles, high quality housing, routine and emergency health care, and saving in case of emergencies.

Climate change impacts populations of concern more significantly than others, including those with low incomes, some communities of color, immigrant groups including those with limited English proficiency, children and pregnant women, older adults, vulnerable occupation groups, persons with disabilities, and persons with pre-existing or chronic medical conditions. In addition to unequal access to resources, extreme weather can lead to social and economic stressors for these groups through disproportionate exposure to climate impacts, thus widening the equity gap.
